literal: being, existing; present participle of √as 'to be'

cognates.note: True cognate, and unusually exact: Skt. sat is the present participle of √as, formed on PIE *h₁s-n̥t-, the very same participial stem behind Greek ón(t)- (‘ontology’), Latin -sēns, and English ‘sooth’ and ‘sin’. The finite verb √as = Latin est = Greek estí = Eng. ‘is’.

invariant_definition: Being or reality, but in technical contexts more than the bare copula 'is'. *Sat* marks what stands up under ontological and causal analysis. The term therefore appears in several linked debates: whether only the ultimate is *sat* in the strict sense or whether dependent realities are also genuinely *sat*; whether effects arise from what already is, as *sat-kārya-vāda* argues, or from prior non-manifestation; and how *sat* differs from both *asat* and, in Advaita, *mithyā*. Upaniṣadic usage gives the term high metaphysical weight, especially in *sad eva somya idam agra āsīt*; later schools then ask what exactly counts as 'what is'. For Advaita, *sat* in the strict sense belongs only to *brahman*. For realist Vedāntas and Sāṅkhya, dependent entities are also real, though not equally independent. A translation that treats *sat* as mere existence misses this layered use in causality, ontology, and soteriology.

school_framing.shared_core: *Sat* names **what stands up under ontological and causal analysis: the determinate referent of *asti***. All schools that use the term agree on this structural role — *sat* is not the bare copula but what survives scrutiny. They differ on (i) whether *sat* in the strict sense belongs only to the unconditioned (Advaita) or also to dependent reals (Viśiṣṭādvaita, Tattva-vāda, Śuddhādvaita, Sāṅkhya, Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, Yoga, Jainism, Trika), (ii) whether effects pre-exist in the cause (*satkārya-vāda*) or arise anew (*asatkārya-vāda*), and (iii) whether the *sat*/*asat* binary is exhaustive (most schools: yes) or admits an *anirvacanīya* third (Advaita).
school_framing.register_axes_note: Schools differ along the M-register axis (which entities carry *sattā* in the strict sense) and the LD-register axis (what *sat* as a predicate can support). **Real disagreement** (AF1) between Advaita (*sat* in the strict sense only of Brahman; *Māṇḍūkya-Bhāṣya* 7) and Tattva-vāda (*sat* of the *bhedapañcaka*; *Anuvyākhyāna* 1.4.111–112) — same M-register, same scope-inside, opposing claims. **Different concept** in Mādhyamaka and Yogācāra: *sat* is framed inside the two-truths / *arthakriyā-sāmarthya* economy, not the substance-attribute economy of the Brāhmaṇical schools.

per_school.definition: In Advaita, *sat* in the strict sense belongs only to the self that is *prapañcopaśama*, *śānta*, and *advaita*; what is ultimately real is that fourth beyond all transactional predication [[MāṇḍU 7](cite://sankara/mandukya-bhasya/upaniṣad.7)] [[GK 2.32](cite://sankara/mandukya-bhasya/2.32)]. Avidyā and the world are therefore not granted unqualified *sattā*; Maṇḍana formulates avidyā as neither *satī* nor *asatī* and hence *anirvacanīyā* [[BS 1.1](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-prose-on-avidy)]. Later Advaita accordingly treats Brahman itself as *satyajñānasukhātmaka*, so that *sat* functions as a *svarūpa-lakṣaṇa* of the non-dual absolute rather than as a predicate shared univocally by appearances [[Advaita-Siddhi 1](cite://madhusudana/advaita-siddhi/1)].
per_school.register_tag: M+E; mumukṣu [REAL-DISAGREEMENT with realist Vedāntas on strict-sense sat]

per_school.definition: In Viśiṣṭādvaita, *sat* is one of the Vedāntic words whose principal referent is Bhagavān Nārāyaṇa, the *paratattva* who stands as the inner controller of all *cit* and *acit* [[Vedārtha-Saṅgraha §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)]. Because the universe is taught through *sāmānādhikaraṇya* with that *śarīrin*, dependent realities are not expelled from being but ordered as his body and mode [[Vedārtha-Saṅgraha §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)]. Vedānta Deśika can therefore gloss *sat* as what is *prāmāṇika* and *upādeya* for the *mumukṣu*, while *asat* is what fails that normative measure [[Sarvārtha-Siddhi ad 1.2b](cite://vedanta-desika/sarvartha-siddhi/ad 1.2b)] [[Sarvārtha-Siddhi ad 1.2c](cite://vedanta-desika/sarvartha-siddhi/ad 1.2c)].

In Tattva-vāda, *sat* is said without reservation of the *prapañca* articulated by *bhedapañcaka*; the fivefold difference is *satya* and beginningless [[Anuvy. 1.4.111-112](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.4.111-112)]. Texts of apparent non-difference are therefore read through *sādṛśya* and dependence, never as *svarūpābhidā*; even in liberation the *jīva* retains qualities similar to the Lord's while remaining distinct [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)]. Madhva's anti-māyāvāda polemic accordingly concludes that, once the rival account fails, *satyatā* belongs to difference itself [[Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana p. 238](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/would instead be true)].
per_school.register_tag: M+S; Tattvavādin [REAL-DISAGREEMENT with Advaita on bhedapañcaka-sattva]

per_school.definition: In Jīva Gosvāmī's *Sat-Sandarbha*, the *abheda-śāstras* are admissible only once Bhagavān's distinction from the *jīva* has been secured and the latter has been understood as a conscious dependent ray upheld by his *svābhāvika-tad-acintya-śakti* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Bhagavān is then fixed as the *parama-svarūpa* of all, as the sun in relation to its rays, so that ontological ultimacy remains personal and devotional rather than collapsing into featureless being [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)]. Once that bheda is settled, *bhajana* alone has *abhidheya*-status [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44)].

In Dvaitādvaita, dependent being is fully real without ceasing to be dependent on Hari: the *jīva* is described as *jñānasvarūpa*, Hari-dependent, atomic, and distinct in each body. [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)] The same self is further said to appear as bound, devotee, and liberated, and its abundance of differentiations is something to be understood rather than dissolved away. [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2)]

For Śuddhādvaita, Brahman is the *samavāyi-kāraṇa* of the world because it pervades the effect as *asti bhāti priyatvena sac-cid-ānanda-rūpeṇa*; *sat* is thus Brahman's own pervasive form within manifestation [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4)]. Names and forms are only *kārya-dharmas*, so the world is not downgraded to illusory appearance but retained as real expression of that same Brahman [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4)]. This is why Vallabha can hold *avikṛta-pariṇāma*: Brahman truly becomes the effect while remaining unmodified in itself [[BSB 1.4.26](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26)].

In Sāṅkhya, *sat* is governed by *satkārya-vāda*: the effect is real in its cause, since what is wholly *asat* cannot be produced, causes are selectively appropriated, and like emerges from like. [[SK 9](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/9)] The classical commentary makes the point explicit as *sad eva sad bhavati*, so *mahad* and the rest are already present in *pradhāna* prior to manifestation and emergence is a disclosure of what is causally there. [[SK 9 comm.](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/9-commentary)]

In Yoga, *sat* marks the persistence of the *dharmin* through quiescent, arisen, and unexpressed *dharmas*, so past and future are not sheer non-being but modes related by *adhva-bheda*. [[YS 3.14](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.14)] [[YS 4.12](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.12)] Vyāsa accordingly says that there is no arising from what is *asat* and no destruction of what is *sat*; causal conditions do not generate a wholly new result but render an already real result present (*vartamānīkaraṇa*). [[YBh 4.12](cite://vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/4.12)]

In Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, *sat* is formalized as *sattā*, a universal present in substance, quality, and motion yet distinct from all three. [[VS 1.2.4-8](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.2.4-8)] Being is therefore not a bare copula but the highest common positive determination under which the school's ontology of *bhāva* is organized. [[VS 1.2.4-8](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.2.4-8)]

In Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, *sat* is treated first in an epistemic register: perception arises only where the senses are in contact with what is present, so *sat* is what is available as *vidyamānopalambha*. [[ŚBh on MS 1.1.4](cite://shabara/shabara-bhasya/1.1.4)] That delimitation is paired with Jaimini's definition of *dharma* as *codanā-lakṣaṇa*; what is ritually efficacious is often not an object of present perception at all, and therefore must be known through Vedic injunction rather than through *pratyakṣa*. [[MS 1.1.2](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.2)] [[ŚBh on MS 1.1.4](cite://shabara/shabara-bhasya/1.1.4)]

Mādhyamaka handles *sat* through the discipline of the two truths: *lokasaṃvṛtisatya* and *paramārthasatya* are both indispensable to dharmadeśanā, but they are not two layers of self-standing being [[MMK 24.8](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.8)]. To miss their *vibhāga* is precisely to miss *tattva* in the Buddha's teaching [[MMK 24.9](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.9)].

For this line, what is *paramārthasat* is whatever is *arthakriyā-samartha*; ultimate reality is marked by causal efficacy [[Pramāṇavārttika 2.3](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.3)]. What lacks that status is *saṃvṛtisat*, the level of conceptual and general construction, so the distinction between the real and the merely conventional is drawn within cognition's own economy [[Pramāṇavārttika 2.2](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.2)] [[Pramāṇavārttika 2.3](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.3)].

In Pratyabhijñā / Trika, *sat* is not inert existence but the Lord's own *sphurattā*, a "great being" beyond spatial and temporal delimitation and identical with reflexive consciousness. [[ĪPK 1.5.14](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.5.14)] Utpaladeva therefore rejects both an *asat* source and any fresh acquisition of being: what is *asat* cannot receive *satsvarūpatā*, and what already is does not become real by later obtaining *sattā*. [[ĪPK 2.4.3](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2.4.3)]

In Jainism, *sat* is whatever is endowed with origination, decay, and persistence, so being is intrinsically many-sided rather than a static unitary essence. [[TS 5.29](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.29)] The same ontology explains why substances are tracked under determinations such as being, number, space, touch, time, interval, mode, and quantity: reality is stable as *dravya* while continually modal as *paryāya*. [[TS 1.8](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.8)] [[TS 5.29](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.29)]

translator_note: Avoid treating *sat* as mere existential vocabulary. English 'being' and 'existence' encourage a thin, quantificational reading, whereas Sanskrit usage often marks robust reality, causal persistence, and resistance to sublation. Also keep *sat* distinct from *satya* ('true') and from the Advaita contrast-term *mithyā*.
